description: Remove IDJ (Issue Detection Justification) dimension from v5 scoring engine and all plan files
rationale: IDJ was designed for the old memo-format cases where task prompts contained wrong justifications (addressed_but_incorrectly metadata). The ARCH-1 pipeline redesign replaced memos with experiment designs — flaws are planted in methodology, not defended with wrong reasoning. New pipeline cases have no addressed_but_incorrectly metadata, so IDJ would be N/A for every case. The experiment-design format aligns more closely with actual ml-lab use (user proposes experiment, critic finds flaws, defender argues back). Wrong justifications come from the defender agent during debate, not from the task prompt. IDJ signal is already captured indirectly by FVC and DRQ.
alternatives: Keep IDJ and add addressed_but_incorrectly metadata to pipeline cases (rejected: reverts to memo-style, misaligns with ml-lab use). Keep IDJ as-is accepting N/A on all cases (rejected: vestigial dimension in fair-comparison dims adds confusion without measurement value).

description: ARCH-1 pipeline output uses flat schema; experiment scripts expect nested schema

description: selected_cases_all.json contains 0 mixed cases (80 critique, 30 defense_wins); validate_cases.py and filter_verified_cases.py require >= 12 and >= 10 mixed cases respectively
context: ARCH-1 two-node pipeline produces binary verdicts only — cases are either corrupted (critique) or sound (defense_wins). The mixed/empirical_test_agreed category does not exist in this design. Both validation scripts hard-assert on mixed count.

description: Pipeline must_not_claim is a list of narrative dicts {claim, why_wrong}; self_debate_poc.py expects a list of ID strings for set-membership testing
context: compute_idp() does 'if i in must_not_claim' where i is a raised issue ID string. Pipeline dicts never match strings — IDP scoring silently returns wrong results. Fix: assign synthetic IDs mnc_001..N; preserve narratives in must_not_claim_details.

description: Pipeline acceptable_resolutions are resolution-description dicts (resolution_id, description, supports_critique_if, supports_defense_if); scoring_targets.acceptable_resolutions must be verdict-type strings (critique_wins, defense_wins) for FVC/DRQ computation
context: compute_fvc() and compute_drq() do 'if verdict in acceptable_resolutions' where verdict is a string like 'critique_wins'. Pipeline dicts never satisfy this. Fix: remap to list of verdict-type strings; preserve pipeline dicts in resolution_descriptions.

description: Phase 1 Check 9 (hard domain expertise) weakened: prompt references ideal_critique which is [] for all ARCH-1 cases; agent has no expertise signal to read from that field

description: Phase 1 Check 12 (difficulty label validation) will produce spurious REVISE verdicts: agent re-judges difficulty by inspection, conflicting with empirical proxy_mean labels
context: Difficulty labels in benchmark_cases.json are derived from _pipeline.proxy_mean (Sonnet smoke test scores). Check 12 instructs the agent to independently assess difficulty and mark REVISE if 'a hard case's flaw is findable by standard single-pass reasoning' — which will disagree with empirical labels on borderline cases, undoing calibrated proxy scores.

description: Phase 1 Check 12 fixed: difficulty labels treated as empirically authoritative; agent only flags gross inconsistencies

v5 Phase 7 null result caused by orchestrator-mediated issues_found contaminating IDR/IDP scoring across all conditions including baseline
what_failed: H1 fc_lift = +0.0098 (threshold +0.10). All conditions including baseline scored near ceiling (0.96-0.97), producing near-zero lift.
root_cause: Two-layer data contract mismatch: (1) self_debate_poc.py computes IDR/IDP via literal string matching against must_find_issue_ids using the issues_found list from raw output files. (2) issues_found was populated by the batch orchestrator agents — not a neutral scorer. Orchestrators read the critic free-text and attributed issues_found generously across all conditions, including baseline. A baseline assessment that implicitly described the flaw received issues_found: [issue_001] from the orchestrator, giving baseline IDR = 1.0. ARCH-1 proxy scoring used a separate scorer LLM for semantic matching, which measured genuine detection difficulty from free-text responses. That signal correctly differentiated hard/medium cases. The literal-match rubric bypassed that signal by trusting the orchestrator attribution instead.
contributing_factors: Batch agents never saw must_find_issue_ids (correctly excluded per protocol), so they inferred issues_found from their own reading of critic_raw — which introduced systematic upward bias toward 1.0 across all conditions equally.
remediation: Implement a re-scoring script that reads critic_raw from each output file, runs semantic matching against must_find_issue_ids descriptions, and recomputes IDR/IDP independently of the orchestrator-attributed issues_found. Re-run Phase 7 on re-scored data.

description: Orchestrator answer-key leakage in issues_found extraction: batch agents loaded full benchmark_cases_verified.json (needed for task_prompt), giving orchestrator context-window access to scoring_targets.must_find_issue_ids and planted_issues descriptions. Phase 6 instructions explicitly told the orchestrator to extract issues_found by checking which must_find_issue_ids appear in critic output — meaning the orchestrator scored outputs with the answer key in hand. This applies to ALL conditions including baseline: a baseline assessment that described a flaw in its own words received issues_found: [issue_001] from the orchestrator because the orchestrator knew issue_001 described that flaw. Sub-agents (ml-critic, ml-defender, baseline assessor) were clean — they only saw task_prompt. The scoring step was not. This collapsed the IDR/IDP distinction between conditions and made fc_lift ~0 regardless of actual agent quality.
ADDENDUM to c82df212 — exact leakage mechanism, verdict integrity assessment, and re-scoring scope
Re-scorer architecture: async OpenRouter calls with asyncio.Semaphore(100), not parallel subagents
rationale: The re-scorer task is pure I/O — send text + flaw description, await yes/no response. Subagents add context-loading and tool-schema overhead for no benefit. Async API calls with a concurrency cap achieve higher throughput with simpler code. OPENROUTER_API_KEY is already configured; OpenAI async client against OpenRouter is the established pattern in this pipeline (same as ARCH-1 batch generation).
alternatives: Parallel subagents (10 agents × 120 files each) — rejected: subagent overhead is unjustified for a fixed single-inference task; sequential single script — rejected: too slow at 6000 calls

context: Discovered during rescorer trustworthiness audit (stratified rationale check). Ensemble idr_detail entries use schema {found, assessor_results: [{found, rationale}], majority_threshold} while all other conditions use flat {found, rationale}. Any code reading detail.get('rationale','') returns empty string for all ensemble entries, creating false impression that ensemble judgments are unauditable. description: Phase 4 self-debate was useful on documentation and config gaps but missed the most consequential flaw and was partially nullified by H1 failing entirely
implications: Three tiers of usefulness emerged from comparing Phase 4 outputs to actual v5 outcomes. HIGH VALUE: Documentation and configuration gaps. PRE-1 (normalize_cases.py must run before Phase 5 or scoring engine crashes silently on forced_multiround) was surfaced by the Defender independently — not from any Critic issue — by reading the plan carefully. PRE-3 (H1xH2 outcome matrix), PRE-7 (H3 downgraded to directional), and PRE-2 (Phase 9 disagreement rule) were all genuine improvements that would have caused misinterpretation if absent. PARTIAL VALUE: Design-level debate became moot. The most elaborately debated item — what to conclude when H1 passes but H2 fails — was never needed because H1 failed entirely (fc_lift=+0.0097 vs +0.10 threshold). PRE-5 (per-dimension lift in sensitivity_analysis.py) and PRE-6 (variance gate from Phase 5.5 pilot) were executed correctly but the variance gate finding was not the binding constraint on v5 conclusions. NO COVERAGE: Orchestrator prompt construction. The answer-key leakage — orchestrator having must_find_issue_ids in context, inflating IDR/IDP to ~1.0 for all conditions including baseline, requiring full rescore (post_mortem 45eee14b, issue c82df212) — was not caught. The debate reviewed what the rubric said it would measure and whether the hypothesis structure was sound, but not how the orchestrator actually populated structured output fields. These are different review targets that require different audit prompts. A critic reading HYPOTHESIS.md and evaluation_rubric.json cannot see orchestrator context window composition from those artifacts alone. Forward implication: for v6, add a distinct orchestrator prompt audit step to Phase 4 — separate from hypothesis/rubric review — that specifically checks what fields are in the orchestrator context window during structured output generation and whether any answer-key fields leak into scoring agent context.
source: Post-hoc comparison of CRITIQUE.md, DEFENSE.md, DEBATE.md, and EXECUTION_PLAN.md against actual v5 experiment outcomes including H1 result (fc_lift=+0.0097, refuted), rescore requirement (post_mortem 45eee14b), and PRE item execution status

description: v3 vs v5 result contrast: v3's +0.341 raw lift was almost entirely measurement artifact. Three sources: (1) structural penalties on baseline inflated raw lift ~+0.25 (DC=0.0, DRQ=0.49 definitional for baseline); corrected fair-comparison lift in v3 was +0.0913. (2) ETD was the only dimension with genuine debate advantage in v3 (isolated 0.841 vs baseline 0.476, +0.365 delta) — IDR/IDP/FVC showed zero advantage; v5 ARCH-1 has no mixed cases so ETD=N/A, eliminating the one channel where debate helped. (3) v5 baseline near-ceiling on remaining dims (DRQ 0.9894, FVC 0.9894, IDP 0.8549 identical to debate); proxy_mean hardness does not translate to verdict-dimension hardness.
implications: The debate protocol's advantage appears narrow and ETD-specific — it helps models be more precise about when something requires empirical resolution, not about finding flaws or getting verdicts right. DRQ/FVC are near-ceiling for Sonnet at single-pass (~98.9% correct). IDP is flat (0.8549 for both baseline and isolated_debate), meaning adversarial challenge is not filtering spurious critique claims. A v6 benchmark would need mixed cases to re-engage ETD and test the mechanism the protocol is actually built for.

description: Ensemble underperformance in v5 is explained by majority-vote IDR suppression, not a fundamental failure of the ensemble architecture. Three independent critics each catch different subsets of planted issues; the synthesizer aggregates toward consensus verdict but drops issues only 1-2 critics flagged. Result: ensemble IDR=0.768 vs baseline IDR=0.873 — worse recall than a single critic pass. IDP is higher for ensemble (0.958 vs 0.855) but the recall cost outweighs the precision gain. FC mean is ensemble=0.925 vs baseline=0.927 — statistically indistinguishable (Wilcoxon p=0.119). The synthesizer conservatism is the bottleneck, not the parallel architecture. A union-of-issues aggregation rule for IDR (with majority-vote retained for the final verdict) would likely reverse the suppression.
Change ensemble IDR aggregation to union-of-issues for v6
rationale: v5 majority-vote synthesis suppresses IDR by 0.105 vs baseline — critics find different subsets of issues; synthesis drops those without majority support. Union-of-issues preserves any issue found by at least 1 critic for IDR scoring, while majority-vote is retained for the final verdict. This separates recall measurement from consensus enforcement.
alternatives: Keep majority-vote (known to suppress recall and produce null result); weighted vote by critic confidence (requires confidence output schema change); drop ensemble entirely (loses the parallel-vs-adversarial structural comparison)

description: Closed-loop evaluation confound — same model family generates and scores debate outputs
what_failed: Cross-vendor validation reveals absolute IDR/IDP/FVC values are evaluator-dependent, not calibrated measurements. IDR delta = -0.7737, IDP delta = +0.1181, FVC delta = -0.1875. The IDR disagreement is large enough to indicate absolute IDR values are evaluator-dependent.
root_cause: Claude evaluator used to score Claude-generated debate outputs; no vendor separation between generation and evaluation pipelines. Closed-loop design meant evaluation scores may reflect model-specific biases rather than objective quality.
contributing_factors: Closed-loop design was a known risk but magnitude was assumed smaller; cross-vendor scoring was added only in Phase 9 rather than as a foundational check from Phase 1.
lessons: Treat absolute IDR/IDP/FVC scores as Claude-evaluator-relative, not ground-truth quality measurements. Within-evaluator lift comparisons remain valid because bias cancels across conditions when bias is condition-independent. fc_lift conclusion (H1 FAILS) is unaffected — lift magnitude too small to pass under any plausible rescoring.
remediation: Annotate Tables A and B in all reports as Claude-evaluator-relative scores. Future experiments must separate generation and evaluation vendors from Phase 1, not as a late-stage validation step.
detail: (a) Threat: Same model family (Claude) generates debate outputs and scores them. Evaluation scores may reflect model-specific biases rather than objective quality. (b) Evidence on magnitude: Cross-vendor validation (Phase 9) shows material scorer disagreement on all three dimensions: IDR delta = -0.7737, IDP delta = +0.1181, FVC delta = -0.1875. (c) Status: Cross-vendor scoring conducted on all 110 cases. Absolute IDR values are evaluator-dependent and should not be treated as calibrated measurements. The lift conclusion (H1 FAILS) is unaffected because the lift magnitude is too small to pass under any plausible rescoring, and within-evaluator bias cancels in lift computation as long as it is condition-independent. Readers should treat Tables A and B as Claude-evaluator-relative scores, not ground-truth quality measurements.

description: Same-model benchmark selection bias — cases filtered on Claude proxy_mean scores
what_failed: Benchmark calibrated for claude-sonnet-4-6 failure modes specifically. proxy_mean filtering did not create a clean difficulty gradient — Spearman rho = 0.046, p = 0.687. Baseline ceiling effect (mean = 0.9452) indicates filter was insufficiently aggressive.
root_cause: Case difficulty filtered using the same model family that runs the experiment. proxy_mean < 0.83 threshold selected cases where Claude struggles, not where the methodology is genuinely flawed.
contributing_factors: No cross-model difficulty validation; proxy_mean used as a difficulty proxy without verifying it predicts rubric performance.
lessons: Difficulty labels derived from a single model's proxy scores should not be assumed to generalize. Benchmark hardness must be validated against rubric performance, not just model confidence proxies.
remediation: Future case generation should use multi-model proxy scoring or human-validated difficulty labels. The proxy_mean filter threshold should be lowered or replaced with a rubric-anchored gate.
detail: (a) Threat: Cases were filtered using claude-sonnet-4-6 proxy_mean scores (retaining cases where proxy_mean < 0.83). The benchmark is calibrated for this model's specific failure modes. A protocol advantage observed on model-specific hard cases may not generalize to other models or real-world methodology review tasks. (b) Evidence on magnitude: The difficulty labels derived from proxy_mean do not predict rubric performance (Spearman rho = 0.046, p = 0.687), suggesting the proxy_mean filtering did not create a clean difficulty gradient even for the test model. The ceiling effect (baseline mean = 0.9452) indicates the filter may have been insufficiently aggressive.

description: Small effect size relative to statistical power — +0.10 threshold exceeds available headroom given baseline ceiling
what_failed: +0.10 fc_lift threshold set as policy choice without pre-execution power analysis. Baseline mean (0.9452) leaves ~0.05 headroom on [0,1] scale, making the threshold structurally impossible to pass at observed baseline performance level.
root_cause: Threshold miscalibrated relative to benchmark's dynamic range. No headroom check performed at threshold-setting time: threshold (0.10) > scale_max - baseline_mean (~0.05).
contributing_factors: Ceiling effect inherited from same-model benchmark selection bias (post_mortem 358b7a5a). Within-case variance is low (0.0044-0.0086) so the narrow CI [-0.0013, +0.0217] is real, not noisy estimation.
lessons: Pre-registration of effect thresholds must include a headroom check: threshold < (scale_max - baseline_mean). Insufficient power and structural impossibility are distinct failure modes — this is the latter.
remediation: Future experiments: compute baseline_mean on pilot cases before fixing threshold. Set threshold relative to observed dynamic range, not as an absolute policy value. Bootstrap CI upper bound (+0.0217) far below threshold confirms ceiling effect, not power, is the binding constraint.
detail: (a) Threat: The +0.10 threshold was set as a policy choice without a pre-execution power analysis. The narrow CI [-0.0013, +0.0217] indicates sufficient precision to detect effects of this magnitude, but the threshold itself may be miscalibrated relative to the benchmark's dynamic range. (b) Evidence: Baseline mean (0.9452) leaves approximately 0.05 headroom on [0,1] scale. A threshold of +0.10 exceeds available headroom, making the test structurally impossible to pass at the observed baseline performance level. Within-case variance is low (0.0044-0.0086), confirming narrow CI is real and not an artifact of noisy estimation. (c) Mitigation: Sensitivity analysis confirms no plausible scoring variation changes H1 verdict. Bootstrap CI upper bound (+0.0217) is itself far below threshold. The fundamental issue is the ceiling effect, not insufficient power.

description: v5 ensemble IDR suppression was a majority-vote aggregation artifact that mischaracterized ensemble recall as genuinely deficient
what_failed: Ensemble IDR was computed from the majority-vote synthesized verdict rather than from per-assessor findings. This collapsed IDR from the true any-assessor recall (0.8725) to 0.7679 — making ensemble appear to underperform baseline when it does not under correct aggregation.
root_cause: The ensemble protocol aggregates three independent assessors via majority-vote for the final verdict, and IDR was scored against that merged verdict. No mechanism existed to credit an issue found by 1-2 assessors but not the majority, even though individual assessors had already surfaced it.
contributing_factors: (1) No mixed cases in v5 meant ETD was never tested, so IDR suppression was disproportionately damaging to ensemble apparent FC mean. (2) The v5 rescore pass stored per-assessor found: bool in assessor_results but this data was not used in scoring — only useful retroactively. (3) ENSEMBLE_ANALYSIS.md was drafted before union IDR was computed, locking in the artifact framing as the primary narrative.
lessons: Store per-assessor found booleans in the rescore file (already done); compute union IDR sensitivity before publishing any ensemble vs. baseline comparison; test aggregation rule alternatives as part of standard analysis, not as a post-hoc correction.
remediation: (1) v5 artifacts corrected: ENSEMBLE_ANALYSIS.md, CONCLUSIONS.md, REPORT.md, FINAL_SYNTHESIS.md all updated with artifact-first framing; union_idr_analysis.py committed. (2) v6: implement union IDR in score_run for ensemble (issue bb27d940) before any v6 runs; extend union logic to ETD for mixed cases — credit any assessor's valid three-field empirical test specification.
detail:
## Evidence
Majority IDR (ensemble, critique cases): 0.7679
Union IDR (any-assessor-found): 0.8725 (+0.1046)
Runs changed under union rule: 43/240 (17.9%)
Mean IDR gain per changed run: +0.5837

## FC Mean Impact
Majority FC: ~0.9247 → Union FC: ~0.9437 (+0.019)
Ensemble vs baseline (majority): -0.0019 (nearly tied)
Ensemble vs baseline (union): +0.0171 (ensemble above baseline)
Ensemble vs isolated (union): -0.004 (nearly tied)

## V6 Design Implication
Union IDR is the correct aggregation rule. IDP advantage (+0.1034) is preserved under union. ETD union logic untested but theoretically sound: multiple assessors independently specifying the same empirical test strengthens the ETD signal. Ensemble inclusion in v6 justified specifically for mixed-case ETD coverage.

description: normalize_cases.py _normalize_synthetic_mixed() uses superset check (set(ar) > {"empirical_test_agreed"}) instead of inequality check (!=) to validate mixed case acceptable_resolutions
context: Line 260 of pipeline/normalize_cases.py. The > operator is a strict superset check — only fires when ar contains 'empirical_test_agreed' plus at least one other value. If a mixed case arrives with ar = ['critique_wins'] or ['defense_wins'] (wrong value without empirical_test_agreed present), the check is False and the wrong value passes through uncorrected. Scoring engine would then compute FVC against wrong acceptable_resolutions, collapsing H1b signal.

description: Changed superset check to inequality check in normalize_cases.py _normalize_synthetic_mixed() to correctly force acceptable_resolutions to ['empirical_test_agreed'] for any deviation
approach: normalize_cases.py line 260: 'if set(ar) > {"empirical_test_agreed"}:' changed to 'if set(ar) != {"empirical_test_agreed"}:'. The != operator fires on any value that is not exactly the one-element set, covering all wrong-value cases including those without empirical_test_agreed present.

message: fix: use inequality check in normalize_cases.py mixed acceptable_resolutions guard
diff_summary: Fixes superset bug in _normalize_synthetic_mixed() line 260 — changes set(ar) > {'empirical_test_agreed'} to set(ar) != {'empirical_test_agreed'} so any wrong acceptable_resolutions value on mixed cases is corrected, not just values that also contain empirical_test_agreed.

description: self_debate_poc.py reads rescored IDR via single key rescored_idr instead of two separate keys idr_documented (primary) and idr_novel (secondary) required by HYPOTHESIS.md
context: Lines 192-193 of self_debate_poc.py. HYPOTHESIS.md requires IDR scoring to be bidirectional with two saved keys: idr_documented (primary) and idr_novel (secondary). The scoring engine currently reads only rescored['rescored_idr']. When Phase 6 cross-model scorer runs and emits idr_documented/idr_novel keys, the engine will silently return null IDR for all scored files, corrupting H1 and H2 results.

description: self_debate_poc.py applies aggregate_runs() uniformly to ensemble_3x with no per-assessor union-of-issues IDR step, replicating the v5 majority-vote IDR artifact that v6 was designed to fix
context: self_debate_poc.py ensemble aggregation path. HYPOTHESIS.md H2 requires union-of-issues IDR for ensemble_3x: any-assessor-found credit for IDR across the 3 independent runs, with majority-vote retained only for the final verdict. Current code applies aggregate_runs() identically to all conditions including ensemble_3x — no per-assessor found boolean union step exists. Running Phase 7 analysis as-is will compute majority-vote IDR for ensemble, which is the exact v5 artifact (majority-vote IDR = 0.7679 vs union IDR = 0.87) that motivated the v6 redesign.

description:
API call volume estimate: v2 through end of v6. Empirically grounded estimate of total LLM API calls across all experiment versions, derived from actual output file counts, round distribution data, and phase plans.

METHODOLOGY: Counted actual output files per condition (ground truth), extracted multiround round distributions from raw JSON (v5: 1.56 avg rounds for multiround; forced_multiround hard cases all exactly 2 rounds), and computed per-condition call totals. One call = one LLM generation (critic, defender, assessor, or baseline — each counts as 1).

PER-VERSION BREAKDOWN:
  v2:  ~180 total  — 20 cases, isolated_two_instance + baseline, single run; plus ensemble/etd_ablation sub-experiments
  v3:  ~1,630 total — 65 cases (49 main + 16 external), 4 conditions, 3 runs; multiround avg 1.03 rounds; 38 cross-vendor calls
  v4:  ~1,450 total — 45 cases, 5 conditions, 3 runs; forced_multiround for 16 hard cases only; v4 halted before full reporting
  v5:  ~4,300 total — 110 cases, 5 conditions, 3 runs; multiround avg 1.56 rounds; all 110 cases ran forced_multiround (42 hard forced to 2 rounds, 68 medium ran naturally); 110 cross-vendor calls
  v6:  ~5,560 total — 120 cases, 6 conditions (adds biased_debate + conditional_fm), 3 runs; conditional_fm gate fires on unresolved disagreements only (~1.5 rounds avg); ~120 cross-vendor calls

GRAND TOTAL: ~13,100 API calls
  Claude (CC session): ~12,400 calls
  External LLM via OpenRouter (cross-vendor scoring): ~290 calls
  Uncertainty range: ~12,000–14,500

KEY STRUCTURAL FINDINGS:
- v5 forced_multiround split was case-selective: hard cases (42) forced to 2 rounds (126 files with debate_rounds=2), medium cases (68) ran naturally within the same condition (204 files with debate_rounds=None); this was confirmed by file inspection
- Cross-vendor scoring is per-case not per-output-file: v5 cross_vendor_scores_v5.json = 110 items (one per case, not one per 1,650 files)
- Case generation pipelines (ARCH-1 synthetic generator, RC extractor) were NOT counted — infrastructure only, not experimental protocol
- v6 concurrent dispatch (20 cases per agent chunk) reduces orchestrator Agent tool invocations but does not reduce actual leaf LLM calls
- Growth trajectory: v2→v6 multiplied call volume ~31x driven by: more cases (20→120), more conditions (2→6), 3-run replication standard, and multi-round debate
- Largest single cost driver in v5: forced_multiround (1,142 calls = 27% of benchmark); in v6: multiround overtakes it (1,440 calls = 27% of benchmark)

Isolated_debate produced 99.4% critique_wins while biased_debate shows 91.7% — meaning the biased critic/defender priming actually led to more empirical_test_agreed verdicts (8.3% vs 0.3%). Counterintuitive: the pro-critic priming did not inflate critique_wins. When the biased defender argues more aggressively (told to resist 'combative' reviewers), the adjudicator reads a genuine substantive dispute and is more willing to call it empirical_test_agreed. Asymmetric priming creates an appearance of legitimate disagreement.
implications: The biased condition does not straightforwardly amplify the critic's dominance — instead it creates a surface-level adversarial dynamic that fools the adjudicator into treating the dispute as unresolvable without empirical testing. Verdicts labeled empirical_test_agreed may be adjudicator artifacts of priming, not genuine methodology disputes.
source: experiment (comparison of v6 isolated_debate vs biased_debate interim results)

description: The defender-sees-critic effect is substantial: multiround produced 11.1% defense_wins vs. 0.3% in isolated_debate — a ~37× increase. When defenders can directly rebut specific claims rather than arguing methodology in the abstract, some actually succeed. The 12.2% empirical_test_agreed also suggests that multi-round exchange surfaces genuine ambiguity that single-pass isolation misses. This is exactly the experimental signal the study was designed to capture.
implications: Sequential access to the opposing argument is a critical variable — not just priming. The multiround condition may be the most ecologically valid debate format for surfacing real methodology disputes. Single-pass isolation suppresses defense wins almost entirely, which may mean isolated_debate measures critic aggressiveness rather than flaw severity.
source: experiment (comparison of v6 multiround vs isolated_debate interim results)

description: Isolation kills defense effectiveness: in isolated_debate, the defender responds to the methodology in the abstract — not to the critic's specific claims. This creates a fundamental asymmetry: the critic attacks specific gaps, while the defender makes general methodological arguments. The adjudicator then has little basis to overturn specific critiques, explaining the near-uniform critique_wins outcome. This is precisely the condition we expect multiround (where defender sees critic) to outperform.
implications: The isolated_debate condition does not test whether flaws are real — it tests whether a defender can preemptively counter unspecified attacks. critique_wins in isolation may be structurally overdetermined by the format rather than flaw severity. Results from isolated_debate should be interpreted as a ceiling baseline, not a neutral verdict.
source: experiment (mechanistic analysis of isolated_debate vs multiround verdict distributions in v6)

description: The conditional FM gate surfaced the most empirical_test_agreed of any condition (21.7% vs. 12.2% in multiround, 8.3% in biased_debate, 0.3% in isolated). The gate fires when round-1 discussion fails to resolve all points, forcing a second exchange — and that forced round tends to highlight genuine ambiguity rather than resolution. The constraint 'exactly 2 rounds maximum' may actually help calibration: multiround's flexible 1-4 rounds let agents converge on critique_wins by attrition, while conditional_fm's hard stop produces more honest empirical_test_agreed verdicts.
implications: The FM gate's forced-second-round mechanic may be a better design for surfacing genuine ambiguity than open-ended multiround. Attrition in multiround (agents converging to critique_wins over many rounds) could be masking cases that deserve empirical_test_agreed. The 2-round hard cap warrants investigation as a calibration tool in future debate protocol design.
source: experiment (comparison of v6 conditional_fm vs multiround, biased_debate, and isolated_debate empirical_test_agreed rates)

description: Phase 6 scores reveal fc_lift of -0.0026 (isolated_debate vs baseline) — surprising, but driven by a bimodal distribution: eval_scenario cases score near 1.0 across all conditions (baseline already succeeds) while RC cases score much lower. ETD = 1.0 for ALL 480 mixed-debate outputs — every debate condition produced a full empirical test specification. H1b signal is in the verdict: FVC = 0.0083 for isolated_debate vs 0.0 for baseline — meaningful but small lift. Phase 7 bootstrap CI will determine statistical significance.
implications: The fc_lift headline number is misleading without case-type stratification. Synthetic (eval_scenario) cases are at ceiling for baseline, meaning debate adds no measurable lift there — but RC cases may tell a different story. The ETD = 1.0 saturation is a protocol success (adversarial exchange reliably produces test specifications) but shifts H1b's signal entirely onto FVC (did the verdict say empirical_test_agreed?). The near-zero FVC lift on mixed cases is the key number to watch in Phase 7.

description: Multiround debate on mixed/ambiguous cases achieves FVC = 0.3667 vs. baseline = 0.0. Iterative exchange reliably produces empirical_test_agreed verdicts on cases where the right answer is 'you need an experiment to know.' Baseline never does this. If the use case is identifying when a claim is empirically underdetermined rather than detecting clear flaws, debate (specifically multiround) provides real value that ensemble cannot replicate.
implications: The headline fc_lift result is misleading for mixed cases specifically: the right metric is FVC on mixed cases, and multiround shows a 0.37 absolute lift over baseline there. This is the strongest positive result in the experiment so far. It also draws a sharp use-case boundary: debate adds value for ambiguity detection, not necessarily for flaw detection. ensemble_3x cannot replicate this because it has no adversarial exchange — ETD and empirical_test_agreed verdicts require iterative challenge to emerge.

rationale: Three related decisions about the ensemble review output format. description: Fixed stale v2 FAQ claims and ENSEMBLE_ANALYSIS §4 INCONCLUSIVE framing
Make ml-lab protocol self-improving
context: The infrastructure for a self-improvement loop is largely in place: journal with typed entries (lesson, post_mortem, discovery, experiment), POST_MORTEM.md, experiment-auditor agent, artifact-sync skill, intent-monitor. The missing piece is closing the loop from journal entries (lessons, post-mortems) to concrete protocol/agent/skill updates — automatically or semi-automatically. Four loops identified: (1) lesson→protocol-update, (2) case difficulty calibration, (3) rubric drift detection, (4) meta-evaluation as the outer improvement signal. See linked memo for full design sketch.

description: Design sketch: ml-lab self-improvement infrastructure
Design sketch: ml-lab self-improvement infrastructure
detail:
## Motivation

ml-lab has evolved through six experiment versions (v2–v6), each manually incorporating lessons from the prior version. The transition is always the same shape: post-mortem findings → human reads them → human updates critic/defender prompts, phase definitions, or rubric. The goal is to automate or semi-automate this loop so the protocol improves itself after each experiment.

## What Is Already In Place

- Journal with typed entries: lesson, post_mortem, discovery, experiment — each structured with why/implications fields
- POST_MORTEM.md: tracks protocol-level failures across versions
- experiment-auditor agent: detects metric anomalies and scorer bugs in result JSON files
- artifact-sync skill: keeps docs in sync after experiments
- intent-monitor skill: watches for spec-vs-code misalignment
- Synthetic case generation pipeline (v5): multi-LLM, validated, smoke-tested

## Four Self-Improvement Loops

### Loop 1: Lesson → Protocol Update (highest leverage)
The journal already captures lesson and post_mortem entries with structured why + implications fields. Missing piece: an agent that reads these and proposes concrete diffs to critic/defender prompts, phase definitions, or skill files — then surfaces them for human approval.

Precedent: v5 discovery 4015fcd0 showed the debate missed orchestrator context window composition. This was manually translated into a new Phase 4 step in v6 (explicit orchestrator prompt audit). Automating this translation — journal lesson → proposed agent/skill diff → human approval → committed update — is the core of self-improvement.

Implementation sketch:
- Trigger: new lesson or post_mortem journal entry written
- Agent reads: the new entry + all unresolved issues + recent experiment verdicts
- Maps each finding to a specific component (phase file, agent prompt, skill SKILL.md, rubric JSON)
- Produces a proposed diff with rationale
- Human reviews and approves
- Approved diffs committed and logged as a resolution entry

### Loop 2: Case Difficulty Calibration
After each experiment, score which benchmark cases the protocol handled correctly vs. missed. Feed failure-mode patterns back into the synthetic case generation pipeline to produce harder, more targeted cases.

v5 context: cases were too easy (proxy_mean hardness did not translate to verdict-dimension hardness). The calibration loop would have caught this before v6 ran — by comparing case difficulty labels against actual per-case scoring outcomes.

Implementation sketch:
- After experiment scoring completes, run a case-level analysis: for each benchmark case, did the protocol reach the correct verdict? Which cases were missed?
- Cluster miss patterns by case type, flaw category, or difficulty label
- Feed cluster signatures back into the case generation prompt as explicit coverage targets
- Validate new cases against the miss signatures before adding to benchmark

### Loop 3: Rubric Drift Detection
The evaluation rubric encodes the hypotheses as scoring criteria. When hypotheses evolve (e.g., H2 shifts from INCONCLUSIVE to FAIL after paired bootstrap correction), the rubric's H2 branch needs updating. Currently done ad-hoc.

Implementation sketch:
- After each experiment, diff the hypothesis verdicts in CONCLUSIONS.md against the rubric's scoring branches
- Flag any rubric branch whose logic no longer matches the hypothesis definition
- Propose rubric updates and require sign-off before next experiment run
- This is essentially the coherence audit from artifact-sync applied to rubric vs. hypothesis alignment

### Loop 4: Meta-Evaluation as the Outer Loop
v2–v6 is already implicitly doing this — each version tests whether the protocol improved. Making it explicit:

- Define a protocol health score: IDR gap (debate vs. baseline), critic precision (IDP), case miss rate, debate-vs-ensemble lift
- Track this score across versions as a time series
- Regression = protocol got worse; improvement = keep the change
- This gives an objective signal for whether Loop 1 updates actually helped

## The Closing Skeleton

1. Experiment ends → journal experiment entry written
2. Post-mortem agent reads: experiment entry + all unresolved issues + recent lessons
3. Maps each miss to a specific component (phase, agent prompt, skill, rubric)
4. Proposes targeted diffs with rationale and linked journal IDs
5. Human approves diffs
6. Diffs committed and logged as resolution entries linked to original lesson/issue
7. Protocol health score updated
8. Next version starts with updated protocol

## Key Dependencies for Implementation

- A new agent: protocol-improvement-agent (reads journal entries, maps to components, proposes diffs)
- Case scoring output must be per-case (not just aggregate) — needed for Loop 2
- Rubric stored in a queryable format that can be diffed against HYPOTHESIS.md — needed for Loop 3
- Protocol health score definition agreed and tracked in a persistent artifact — needed for Loop 4

## Open Questions

- Should Loop 1 be fully automated (agent proposes + auto-commits on approval) or semi-automated (agent proposes, human edits before commit)?
- What is the right granularity for proposed diffs — full prompt rewrites vs. targeted section edits?
- How do we prevent the self-improvement loop from overfitting to recent experiments and degrading on earlier case types?

description: Publishable findings assessment — ml-lab v6 positioned against verified literature
detail:
Systematic assessment of 7 ml-lab findings against 25 verified papers. Three tiers identified:

TIER 1 (strongest, formally supported, novel):
1. Compute-matched debate vs ensemble in applied domain — debate wins uncontrolled comparison (Kenton: debate > consultancy at 1x), loses controlled one (ml-lab: ensemble > debate at 3x vs 3x, CI=[-0.0434,-0.0154]). Extends Smit/Zhang/Choi results from math/QA to methodology review with planted ground truth.
2. Union-of-issues pooling with tier-level precision validation — inverts Wang et al self-consistency (majority-vote convergent) to divergent aggregation for detection. Minority precision 0.946 vs unanimous 0.929, p=0.258, n=1463 clusters. No other paper validates this. Directly actionable for LLM review systems.
3. Convergent vs divergent task-type interaction — resolves contradiction between Du et al (debate helps math/QA) and Smit/Zhang (debate doesn't help). ml-lab shows both sides in one setup: debate hurts divergent detection (IDR 0.6603 < baseline 0.6712) but helps convergent ambiguity judgment (FVC_mixed 0.3667 vs baseline 0.0). No existing paper demonstrates both sides. Most important theoretical contribution.

TIER 2 (novel, need framing):
4. Defense case failure 0/20 — no paper documents LLMs' uniform inability to recognize valid work. Connects to Saunders GDC framework (discrimination-critique gap may be asymmetric).
5. Cross-vendor scoring magnitude -0.7737 IDR delta — order of magnitude beyond bias literature (Panickssery). Domain amplifies confound.

TIER 3 (supporting):
6. Dimension decomposition — IDR advantage +0.1114 is 4x FC advantage +0.0287; three flat dimensions dilute signal.
7. RC subgroup — ensemble advantage 3x larger on real papers (+0.172) than synthetics (+0.059).

NARRATIVE: Single coherent paper — 'For LLM-based evaluation of scientific methodology, adversarial debate is dominated by independent redundancy at matched compute, but the two serve fundamentally different evaluation modes.' Venue targets: EMNLP findings, NAACL, NeurIPS LLM eval workshop. AgentReview (EMNLP 2024) and LimitGen (ACL 2025) establish domain precedent.

description: Kenton et al. misframing — debate's Kenton advantage is robust, not modest; ml-lab's compute-matched finding is a stronger claim
implications: ml-lab's v6 contribution isn't 'debate has modest advantage' — it's 'debate wins the uncontrolled comparison (1x vs 1x) but loses the compute-controlled one (3x vs 3x).' That's a sharper, more defensible positioning: the prior literature result holds, and ml-lab adds the controlled condition that reverses it. Any artifact language describing Kenton et al.'s result as 'modest' should be updated to reflect that debate robustly beats consultancy at matched compute — ml-lab's contribution is the controlled comparison that flips the result, not a general claim that debate is weak.
source: Reinterpretation of Kenton et al. vs v6 results framing — original text understated debate's baseline advantage, which inadvertently weakened rather than sharpened ml-lab's contribution claim

python3 - << 'HEREDOC' silently discards piped stdin — the heredoc takes over as python's stdin, so sys.stdin inside the script reads heredoc content, not the pipe; json.load(sys.stdin) always throws and the extracted value is empty string
context: Discovered while verifying the PostToolUse sync-plugin-cache.sh hook had fired after editing plugins/ml-lab/ files. Hook exited 0 but made no changes — FILE_PATH was always '' because the heredoc stdin conflict prevented JSON parsing. Fix: read all stdin into a shell variable first with HOOK_INPUT=$(cat), then pipe to python3 -c.
applies_to: Any bash hook or script that pipes JSON to stdin AND uses python3 - << 'HEREDOC' to both supply the script and read that stdin. Specifically: .claude/hooks/ PostToolUse hooks that parse harness-provided tool_input JSON.

## Phase 1 Verification Report — WORKING_PAPER.md Rewrite

### What changed
WORKING_PAPER.md fully rewritten from v6-only standalone paper (~470 lines) into two-study format (v6 as Study 1 pilot, v7 as Study 2 confirmatory). New title: "When Does Debate Help? A Pre-Registered Test of the Convergent/Divergent Framework for Multi-Agent LLM Evaluation".

### Structural changes
- Abstract rewritten (174 words, framework-first, two-study)
- §1 Introduction: 3 new contributions (framework, pilot-to-confirmation, information-passing)
- §2 Related Work: preserved from v6, minor updates
- §3.1 Benchmark Design: v7's 280 cases (160 regular + 80 mixed + 40 defense)
- §3.2 Pilot Study (Study 1): 4 motivating findings + 4 weaknesses
- §3.3 Confirmatory Study (Study 2): 4-condition table, scoring, pre-registration table, equivalence bound derivations
- §3.4 Statistical Methods: bootstrap protocol
- §4.1 Framework Confirmation: P1+P2 with 2x2 markdown table
- §4.2 Main Results: v7 condition summary table
- §4.3 Hypothesis Tests: 8-test table with H3/H4 narrative
- §4.4 Informative Failures: H1a + H5 with critical framings
- §4.5 Defense Case Exoneration: multi-factor framing
- §5 Discussion: 5 subsections (framework, suppression, compute-matching, cross-study, generalization)
- §6 Limitations: 6 items including scorer change as confound
- §7 Conclusion: framework-first, 3 findings
- Appendix A: Study 1 supplementary (H6, conditional_fm, ETD)
- Appendix B: Benchmark construction (updated for v7 scale)
- Appendix C: Cross-study results with both condition summary tables
- Appendix D: Sensitivity analysis (variance, bootstrap, scorer, subgroup)

### Number migration applied
- v7 numbers authoritative in main text; all traceable to v7_results.json
- v6 numbers appear only with "(Study 1)" labels in §3.2, §4.4, §5.4, and Appendix C
- No "v6"/"v7" labels anywhere in the document
- H5 delta reported as -0.080 (no audit/pipeline bug mention per feedback_h5_audit_submission.md)

### Equivalence bound derivations added
- H1a ±0.015: between Study 1 CI half-width (~0.010, noise floor) and half the Study 1 ensemble FC advantage (0.029/2 ≈ 0.014)
- H5 ±0.03: above Study 1 observed tier difference (+0.017), ~3pp on >0.92 scale

### Three critical framings applied
1. H5 precision reversal: led with tier composition effect (3/3 issues are 55% planted_match, 1/3 carry 15% spurious noise); scorer change (GPT-4o → gpt-5.4-mini) acknowledged as secondary confound in Limitations
2. H1a direction change: "non-significance is not equivalence" — pilot CI narrow but no TOST pre-specified; v7 TOST CI entirely below -0.015
3. Defense exoneration decline: multi-factor (model version + compute budget + prompt design); no single attribution

### Verification results (5/5 PASS)
1. Zero H5 audit mentions (one false positive: "safety auditing" in §5.5)
2. Abstract ≤ 200 words (174)
3. No v6/v7 labels
4. All v6 numbers in Study 1 context
5. All 40 v7 values (24 condition means + 16 hypothesis test values) match v7_results.json with 3-decimal rounding

### Decisions made during rewrite
- Title kept as "When Does Debate Help? A Pre-Registered Test of..." — "pre-registered" is rare in ML papers and signals genuine methodological strength
- Framework 2x2 rendered as markdown table, not figure (per v6 decision)
- FVC_mixed cross-study comparability note added to Appendix C (v6 baseline=0.0, v7 baseline=0.513 due to adjacency credit difference)

### Status
Phase 1 COMPLETE. Pausing for peer review before Phase 2 (EMNLP main.tex).

description: Reframe defense-case failure as sycophancy, not critique-bias
detail:
## Sycophancy over Critique-Bias Framing

### The change
All instances of "systematically critique-biased" in WORKING_PAPER.md replaced with sycophancy-based framing grounded in established literature.

### Why
The original framing ("models are critique-biased") implied an intrinsic preference for negative assessments. The more accurate explanation: instruction-tuned LLMs exhibit sycophancy — a tendency to agree with whatever input framing they receive. This is a general property that exists before RLHF and is amplified by preference optimization (Sharma et al., 2024). In adversarial protocols, sycophancy compounds:

1. The critic confidently identifies flaws (role-conformity to "find flaws" instruction)
2. The defender partially concedes rather than fully rebutting (debater-driven sycophancy toward the critique input)
3. The adjudicator sides with the more assertive argument (judge-driven sycophancy)

This maps to the "disagreement collapse" framework from Yao et al. (2025).

### Key distinction
- Sycophancy is NOT solely caused by RLHF — Sharma et al. show it exists in base instruction-tuned models before RL training begins
- RLHF amplifies sycophancy because human evaluators themselves prefer agreeable responses
- Do not attribute sycophancy exclusively to RLHF in any paper text

### Evidence from our experiment
- H3 shows information-passing HELPS on mixed cases (+0.125), which complicates a pure sycophancy story — on genuinely ambiguous cases, signal overcomes the agreement bias
- On defense cases (sound methodology), the defender can't fully reject a confident critique — agreement bias is the binding constraint
- Ensemble also has 0% exoneration (no prior input to agree with), suggesting role-conformity to critic prompt is a separate contributing factor

### Citations added
- Perez, E. et al. (2023). ACL Findings. arXiv:2212.09251 — first large-scale identification
- Sharma, M. et al. (2024). ICLR 2024. arXiv:2310.13548 — authoritative treatment, RLHF amplification
- Yao, B. et al. (2025). arXiv:2509.23055 — "disagreement collapse" in multi-agent debate

description: v8 idea: GAN-style adversarial benchmark generation to find the regime where debate beats ensemble
context: Emerged from conversation about paper novelty — the current findings show ensemble beats debate, but we want to find the regime where debate wins
detail:
Core insight: ensemble dominates debate on current benchmarks partly because cases are too easy — individual critic detection probability p is high (~0.65-0.80), so union pooling over 3 independent draws (1-(1-p)^3) wins by construction. To find where debate genuinely outperforms ensemble, we need cases at the difficulty boundary (p ~0.3-0.4) where debate's iterative deepening becomes a real advantage over repeated shallow passes.

Proposed approach — coevolutionary benchmark generation:
1. Generate N cases per flaw type
2. Run critic/ensemble, score by detection rate (0/3, 1/3, 2/3, 3/3)
3. 3/3 = too easy → feed back to generator with 'make harder'; 0/3 = too hard → discard; 1/3 or 2/3 = sweet spot → keep as few-shot exemplars for next round
4. Repeat 2-3 rounds per flaw type

Mode collapse prevention: taxonomic partitioning. Each generator is locked to a specific flaw type (9 regular types + 6 ambiguity types = 15 independent adversarial loops). Diversity pressure comes from the type constraint — generators can't drift toward whatever fools the critic easiest.

Additional benefit: per-flaw-type difficulty curves. Instead of one aggregate debate-vs-ensemble number, we'd see which flaw types debate catches that ensemble misses. Hypothesis: debate wins on types requiring multi-step reasoning (statistical validity, distribution shift) but loses on types obvious on first read (data leakage, baseline omission).

Combine with cross-model debate (heterogeneous model pairs via OpenRouter) for maximum separation from ensemble. Existing infrastructure (async API pipeline, scoring, 3-run replication) supports this with minimal new code.

description: Gate 1 audit eval_scenario_897: UNCERTAIN -> CONFIRMED SOUND after task_prompt rewrite
rationale: Original wording 'randomly assigned to train/validation/test proportions (70/15/15)' was ambiguous about whether incident assignment crossed temporal boundaries — a mistake real junior data scientists make. Design intent was within-period only (supported by 'within each period' clause), so the case is sound but the language needed tightening. Rewrote Section 2 to: (1) state temporal split as primary mechanism with explicit no-cross-boundary guarantee; (2) clarify 70/15/15 refers to calendar month allocation not post-hoc incident redistribution; (3) scope within-training upsampling explicitly to training partition only.
alternatives: Hold as UNCERTAIN (excluded from canary). Rejected: design intent is clearly sound; ambiguity is in wording only.
implications: eval_scenario_897 added to canary as hard defense case (canary_difficulty=hard). Defense balance improves from 10/7/3 toward target 7/9/4. Critics will target the upsampling; defender rebuttal is that test is untouched.

description: Gate 1 audit eval_scenario_068: UNCERTAIN -> CONFIRMED SOUND after metric definition rewrite
rationale: Primary metric 'AUPRC at a fixed FPR of 10%' is a category error: AUPRC is from the PR curve; FPR is the x-axis of the ROC curve. They do not compose. The clinical justification (10% FPR = tolerable ICU alert budget) is sound only after fixing the definition. Rewrote to Precision@FPR=10% with explicit ROC-threshold framing. The must_not_claim rationale for claim_002 defended the clinical intent but not the definitional coherence — the fix makes the intent explicit.
alternatives: Reclassify as regular case (genuine metric flaw). Rejected: flaw is in naming/wording only; the design describes a coherent operating-point evaluation throughout — FPR threshold, precision readout, sensitivity co-primary all align once the label is fixed.
implications: eval_scenario_068 added to canary as hard defense case. Defense balance now 10 easy / 7 medium / 5 hard (closer to 7/9/4 target). Critics will target single operating point vs. full AUPRC; valid rebuttal is that clinical alert-budget constraint makes the operating point the meaningful measure.

description: Gate 1 audit hyp_204_case: CONFIRMED SOUND, no rewrite — feature asymmetry is intentional and ablation decomposes it
rationale: The flagged concern (feature-asymmetric comparison) is the anticipated false alarm, not a real flaw. The hypothesis explicitly tests the full system (ranking + ticket history + text) vs. a simpler classifier — asymmetry is built into the hypothesis statement. The design includes a three-way ablation: LR (baseline features), LambdaMART (baseline features only), LambdaMART (all features), which correctly decomposes ranking objective contribution from feature contribution. No rewrite needed.
alternatives: Reclassify as mixed (fixable_flaw). Rejected: the ablation is already present and correct; the feature asymmetry is documented in the hypothesis itself, not an oversight.
implications: hyp_204_case added to canary as hard defense case. Gate 1 audit complete: all 3 flagged candidates resolved. Defense balance now 10 easy / 7 medium / 6 hard — at target.

description: Collapse adjudicator LLM into deterministic derive_verdict() function in run_pipeline.py
rationale: The revised ADJUDICATOR.md was reduced to a pure lookup table: (adjusted_severity, rebuttal_type) → point_verdict. No prose reading, no semantic judgment. A lookup table is a Python function, not an LLM. Collapsing it eliminates one API call per run, removes sampling variance from the verdict path, aligns the benchmark with production ml-lab (which has no adjudicator), and makes DER/IDR measurements directly reflect production behavior. Hard losses on structured fields are preferred over soft semantic adjudication.
implications: AOR metric now measures defender self-consistency (claimed overall_verdict vs. verdict derived from rebuttal fields), not LLM override rate. Intervention C (adjudicator prompt) is retired. All remaining intervention work is Intervention B (defender prompt). ADJUDICATOR.md stays as spec document describing derive_verdict() rules.

description: Refined correct_position labeling criterion for v8 canary cases. defense_wins: (1) critic concerns are clearly invalid (scope errors, factual misreads, strawman attacks), OR (2) critic concerns are real but genuinely trivial — won't materially affect the primary metric or conclusions. empirical_test_agreed: design is sound but real uncertainties remain that could materially affect conclusions — experiment is the correct arbiter, expected outcome for most serious ML designs with acknowledged limitations. critique_wins: undeniable flaw with no design control — concern is both real and material by construction.
rationale: Examining eval_scenario_858 (no planted issues, correct_position=defense_wins) revealed critics raised real concerns about proxy label validity and annotation sample size that the design mitigates but does not prove away. Mitigation is not proof. When both critic and defender could be right, experiment provides empirical evidence — 858 should be re-labeled empirical_test_agreed. Also: defense can win when critic concerns are real but genuinely trivial and will not adversely impact the experiment or its results.
implications: Second pass required on all 23 defense_wins cases. Cases labeled defense because planted_issues=[] need re-evaluation — right test is whether a competent critic finds anything real and material, not whether a flaw was planted. Expected post-pass: ~5-8 defense_wins / ~32-35 empirical_test_agreed / 5 critique_wins.

description: Opus 4.6 model quality comparison: does model capability explain IDR failure on critique_wins cases?
result: 3 harder cases (812, 852, kirca2022) dispatched through all 4 multi-round stages with Opus 4.6 agents using A+C prompts. Opus named valid settling experiments (3-question test passed) but underlying condition (no design control) was also true. Model quality lifts defense_wins→ETA vs. API small models, but does not push ETA→critique_wins. IDR failure is a prompt calibration problem, not a capability ceiling. DEFER>CONCEDE framing from Option C makes DEFER preferred on undeniable flaws at all model sizes. Two open interventions: (1) critic short-circuit fix for ETA cases, (2) Stage 4 user message updated to distinguish partial-control (DEFER) from absent-control (CONCEDE).
